finding_results.evidence: chapters/chapter_16.md:155 now gives Lola a winding, story-shaped entry (observation about Marcus at the front desk last Tuesday, switchback to 'the whole time you were already doing this other thing'); chapters/chapter_16.md:161 replaces flat 'You didn't give us the chance' with self-correcting 'You didn't even — mijo, you didn't even ask'; chapters/chapter_16.md:185 replaces generic 'Mijo, please' with question-led indirection ('do they know you? Not your number. Do they know —') trailing into 'Mijo' when words fail.
finding_results.notes: Both exchange windows now use Lola's style-bible traits: question-led approach, indirect arrival at the point through observation/adjacent concern, self-correction switchbacks, braided speech, endearment anchoring, and high contraction level. Marcus's existing responses ('I'm sorry I didn't tell you' and 'I can't do this for love') now answer something only Lola would say rather than generic objections. Line 219 ('Ay, Dios mio') left unchanged — it functions as compressed exit affect after the fuller exchanges, not as her primary voice in the scene.

summary: Revised Lola's two main speaking exchanges to restore her style-bible voice: indirect, question-led, braided, self-correcting, and endearment-rich. First exchange (line 155) now opens with a winding observation about Marcus visiting last week before pivoting to the betrayal; her follow-up (line 161) uses a characteristic mid-sentence restart. Second exchange (line 185) replaces a flat plea with questions about whether his new contacts know him as a person, trailing off into the endearment when the thought exceeds what she can finish. Scene outcome, leverage shifts, and all other characters' lines unchanged.
